Lab 1: The Apollo Missions¶

Introduction¶

Imagine you are an engineer working on the very early days of the Apollo program. In order to send people to the moon, you need a basic understanding of the gravitational potential and forces the mission will experience. You also need to start quantifying the performance of the new rocket that will carry the capsule: the Saturn V.

Your team at NASA has been tasked with conducting a series of investigations, which you are guided through below. You will make some plots, and also write some short paragraphs with your scientific conclusions. The NASA Director Gene Kranz will use your notebook to defend NASA funding to congress. So your answers should be short and to the point, quantitative in nature, and contain highly polished plots, with appropriate titles, units, etc.

Other important requirements:

  • Your final notebook---BOTH the .ipynb file AND the html export---should be COMMITTED and PUSHED to your fork of the PHYS265-spring26 GitHub repository. Instructions for doing this are available online.
  • All plotting should be done in object oriented fashion, as discussed in class. Any use of “plt.plot()” will result in zero points being awarded for that question.
  • Before submitting your work, reset JLD and then re-run your final code (Kernel → Restart Kernel and Run All Cells…) Make sure your code executes as intended and without error. The first cell should be "1", and the cells should execute in order.

A table of relevant physical constants is below. Good luck!

Quantity Value
Gravitational Constant ($G$) $6.67 \times 10^{-11}$ m³/kg/s²
Gravitational Acceleration ($g$) 9.81 m/s²
Mass of the Earth ($M_E$) $5.9 \times 10^{24}$ kg
Mass of the Moon ($M_M$) $7.3 \times 10^{22}$ kg
Mass of Apollo Command Module 5500 kg
Radius of the Earth ($R_E$) 6378 km
Radius of the Moon ($R_M$) 1737 km
Distance from Earth to Moon ($d_{EM}$) $3.8 \times 10^{8}$ m
Exhaust Velocity of Saturn V Stage 1 (S-1C) ($v_e$) $2.4 \times 10^{3}$ m/s
Burn Rate of S-1C ($\dot{m}$) $1.3 \times 10^{4}$ kg/s
Wet Mass of S-1C ($m_0$) $2.8 \times 10^{6}$ kg
Dry Mass of S-1C ($m_f$) $7.5 \times 10^5$ kg
In [1]:
#show
# In the code cell beneath this one you can put your imports,
# and define any variables you might need (e.g. the mass of the earth)
In [2]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import math
from scipy.integrate import quad
%matplotlib ipympl 

G    = 6.67 / 10**(11) # m^3/kg /s^2
g    = 9.81            # m/s^2
M_E  = 5.9*10**24      # kg
R_E  = 6378000.0       # m
d_EM = 3.8 * 10**8     # m 
M_M  = 7.3 * 10**22    # kg
R_M  = 1.737 * 10**6   # m
m    = 1.3 * 10**4     # kg/s
m0   = 2.8 * 10**6     # kg
mf   = 7.5 * 10**5     # kg
ve   = 2.4 * 10**3     # m/s
M_ap = 5500.0          # kg

Part 1: The Gravitational Potential of the Earth¶

The gravitational potential at a distance $r$ from a mass $M$ is given by:

$$\Phi(r) = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

where G is the gravitational constant.

  1. Code up a function that takes as an input the mass of a body $M$ , the cartesian location of the mass $(x_M , y_M)$, and the location of some distant evaluation point $(x, y)$. Have the function return the potential at the evaluation point. You may consider approximating the Earth as a point particle, but in which case, be careful! For in that approximation, the the potential contains a singularity. In our function, use all modern best practices for coding. E.g. type hints, type checks, pre and post conditions, doc strings, etc.
In [3]:
def f(M,xm,ym,x,y):
    """ Function for calculating the gravitational potential of a point some distance away from a mass.
    args:
    M: mass of the object kg (float)
    xm: x location of the mass m (float)
    ym: y location of the mass m (float)
    x: x location of the test point m (float)
    y: y location of the test point m (float)
    out put (Phi) the gravitational feild strength at the point tested m^2/s^2 (float) 
    """
    if M > M_M:
        R_tol = R_E #gives seperate tolerances for if the calculation is for the earth or moon
    else:
        R_tol = R_M
    if not isinstance(M, float) and not isinstance(M, int):
        raise TypeError(f"Mass needs to be a float or int {type(M)}") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(xm, float) and not isinstance(xm, int):
        raise TypeError(f"Position needs to be a float or int") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(ym, float) and not isinstance(ym, int):
        raise TypeError(f"Position needs to be a float or int") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(x, float) and not isinstance(x, int):
        raise TypeError(f"Position needs to be a float or int") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(y, float) and not isinstance(y, int):
        raise TypeError(f"Position needs to be a float or int") # gives error
        
    elif np.sqrt((x-xm)**2+(y-ym)**2) < R_tol:
        Phi = np.nan # prevents the function from dividing by zero
        
    elif M <0: # negative mass does not exist 
        raise ValueError(f"Mass must be positive") 
        
    else: # main calculation
        r = np.sqrt((x-xm)**2+(y-ym)**2)
        Phi = -G*M/r
        
        # Post conditions
        assert Phi < 0, " Phi(r) should always be negative"
        assert isinstance(Phi, float), " Phi(r) should be a float"
    return(Phi)
  1. Make a 1D plot of the absolute value of the gravitational potential $|\Phi|$ as a function of distance $x$ from the surface of the Earth, assuming $y = 0$. Plot out to $1.5\times$ the distance from the Earth to the Moon. Because the potential falls quickly with r, make the y-axis logarithmic to visualize it better.
In [4]:
n = 100

x1 = np.linspace(0,1.5*d_EM,n) # x range
y = np.zeros(n) # y range, for this y is zero

F = np.vectorize(f)


fig, ax = plt.subplots()
y1 = abs(F(M_E,0.0,0.0,x1,0.0))
line = ax.plot(x1, y1)
ax.set_yscale('log')
ax.set_title(' magnitude of the gravitational potential at distance x away from the earth')
ax.set_ylabel('gravitational potential 2log(m/s)')
ax.set_xlabel('X distance m')
Out[4]:
Text(0.5, 0, 'X distance m')
Figure
  1. Then, make a 2D color-mesh plot of the potential $\Phi$ with the Earth at the origin. Plot $|\Phi|$ over a large range of $x$ and $y$, for example, from $-1.5d_{EM}$ to $+1.5d_{EM}$ . Force the plot to have a square aspect ratio. Make sure to add a nice colorbar, and make the colorbar logarithmic.
In [5]:
n = 1000

x = np.linspace(-1.5*d_EM,1.5*d_EM,n)
y = np.linspace(-1.5*d_EM,1.5*d_EM,n)
xprobe,yprobe = np.meshgrid(x,y)

color = np.log(abs(F(M_E,0.0,0.0,xprobe,yprobe)))
fig, ax1 = plt.subplots()
mesh = ax1.pcolormesh(xprobe,yprobe, color, cmap='plasma')
ax1.set_aspect("equal")
cbar = fig.colorbar(mesh)

# plot labels
ax1.set_title(' color plot of the gravitational potential of the earth')
ax1.set_ylabel('Y distance m')
ax1.set_xlabel('X distance m')
cbar.set_label('gravitational potential log(m^2/s^2)')
Figure

Part 2: The Gravitational Potential of the Earth-Moon System¶

Now, imagine the Earth is at the origin $(x_{E},y_{E}) = (0,0)$, and the moon is at a location $(x_{M},y_{M}) = \big( \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}} \big)$.

  1. Make an updated 2D color-mesh plot, from $-1.5d_{EM}$ to $+1.5d_{EM}$, of the gravitational potential of the combined Earth-Moon system.
In [6]:
M_xy = d_EM/np.sqrt(2)

color1 = np.log(abs(F(M_E,0.0,0.0,xprobe,yprobe)+F(M_M,M_xy,M_xy,xprobe,yprobe)))
fig, ax2 = plt.subplots()
mesh_EM = ax2.pcolormesh(xprobe,yprobe, color1, cmap='plasma')
ax2.set_aspect("equal")
cbar = fig.colorbar(mesh_EM)

# plot labels
ax2.set_title('color plot of the gravitational potential of the earth and moon')
ax2.set_ylabel('Y distance m')
ax2.set_xlabel('X distance m')
cbar.set_label('gravitational potential log(m^2/s^2)')
Figure
  1. Additionally, make a 2D contour plot over the same $x$ and $y$ range. Do not let all the contours bunch up around the Earth and Moon. You will probably again need to tell matplotlib to use a logarithmic color scale.
In [7]:
fig, ax3 = plt.subplots()
contour_EM = ax3.contour(xprobe,yprobe, color1, cmap='plasma', levels = 15)
ax3.set_aspect("equal")
cbar = fig.colorbar(contour_EM)
fig.tight_layout()

# plot labels
ax2.set_title('contour plot of the gravitational potential of the earth and moon')
ax2.set_ylabel('Y distance m')
ax2.set_xlabel('X distance m')
cbar.set_label('gravitational potential log(m^2/s^2)')
Figure

Part 3: The Gravitational Force Field of the Earth-Moon System¶

The gravitational force $\vec{F}$ that a mass $M_1$ exerts on a mass $m_2$ is:

$$\vec{F}_{21} = -G \frac{M_1 m_2}{|\vec{r}_{21}|^2} \hat{r}_{21}$$

where $\vec{r}_{21}$ is the displacement vector from $M_1$ to $m_2$.

  1. Code up a function that takes as an input the mass of a body $M_1$, the mass of a secondary body $m_2$, the cartesian location of the first mass $(x_1, y_1)$, and the cartesian location of the second body $(x_2, y_2)$. Have the function return the two gravitational force components, $F_x$ and $F_y$, experienced by the mass $m_2$ from the force exerted by $M_1$. Note that the gravitational force contains a singularity, and so if you made the approximation that the Earth is a finite point, you will need to avoid it. Like for the 1D potential, make sure it follows modern best-coding practices.
In [8]:
def fxy(M1,m2,x1,y1,x2,y2):
    """ Function for calculating the gravitational force of a object some distance away from a mass.
    args:
    M1: mass of the larger object kg (float)
    m2: mass of the smaller object kg (float)
    x1: x location of the mass m (float)
    y1: y location of the mass m (float)
    x2: x location of the test mass m (float)
    y2: y location of the test mass m (float)
    out put: Fx,Fy the x and y components of gravitational force vector N (float) 
    """
    if M1 > M_M:
        R_tol = R_E #gives seperate tolerances for if the calculation is for the earth or moon
    else:
        R_tol = R_M
    if not isinstance(M1, float) and not isinstance(M1, int):
        raise TypeError(f"Mass needs to be a float or int") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(m2, float) and not isinstance(m2, int):
        raise TypeError(f"Mass needs to be a float or int {type(M)}") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(x1, float) and not isinstance(x1, int):
        raise TypeError(f"Position needs to be a float or int") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(y1, float) and not isinstance(y1, int):
        raise TypeError(f"Position needs to be a float or int") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(x2, float) and not isinstance(x2, int):
        raise TypeError(f"Position needs to be a float or int") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(y2, float) and not isinstance(y2, int):
        raise TypeError(f"Position needs to be a float or int") # gives error
        
    elif np.sqrt((x1-x2)**2+(y1-y2)**2) < R_tol:
        Fx = np.nan # prevents the function from dividing by zero
        Fy = np.nan 
        
    elif M1 <0:
        raise ValueError(f"Mass must be positive")
        
    elif m2 <0:
        raise ValueError(f"Mass must be positive")
        
    else: 
        r     = np.sqrt((x1-x2)**2+(y1-y2)**2)
        theta = np.atan2((y1-y2),(x1-x2))
        Fx    = -(G*M1*m2/(r**2))*np.cos(theta)
        Fy    = -(G*M1*m2/(r**2))*np.sin(theta)
        
        # Post conditions
        assert isinstance(Fx, float), " Fx should be a float"
        assert isinstance(Fy, float), " Fy should be a float"
    return(Fx,Fy)

FXY = np.vectorize(fxy) # vectorizes function for use over a range
  1. Assuming the Earth is still at the origin and the Moon is still at $(x_M, y_M) = \big( \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}} \big)$, make a 2D streamplot of the gravitational force the Earth-Moon system will exert on the Apollo 11 command module. Plot the force over the same x-y range of $-1.5 d_{EM}$ to $+1.5 d_{EM}$. Add a nice color bar, which will again probably need to be logarithmic. Mark the location of the earth with a black circle, and the moon with a black square. Add a legend.
In [9]:
FEx , FEy = FXY(M_E,M_ap,0.0,0.0,xprobe,yprobe) # returns the x and y components of the force from the earth on the apollo command shuttle
FMx , FMy = FXY(M_M, M_ap, M_xy,M_xy, xprobe, yprobe) # same as above for the moon
color2 = np.log(np.sqrt(((FEx+FMx)**2+(FEx+FMy)**2)))

fig, ax4 = plt.subplots()

# visuals for earth and moon
ax4.plot([0], [0], "ko", label = 'earth')
ax4.plot([M_xy], [M_xy], "ks", label = 'moon')

# Main plot
Stream_EM = ax4.streamplot(xprobe,yprobe, -FEx-FMx,-FEy-FMy, color = color2, cmap='nipy_spectral')
ax4.set_aspect("equal")
cbar = fig.colorbar(Stream_EM.lines)
fig.tight_layout()

# plot labels
ax4.legend(loc='lower right');
ax4.set_title('Vector stream plot of the gravitational force of the earth moon system')
ax4.set_ylabel('Y distance m')
ax4.set_xlabel('X distance m')
cbar.set_label('gravitational force log(N)')
Figure
  1. You will note there is a point between the Earth and the Moon where the field lines seem to vanish. This is the point where the Earth's and Moon's gravitational forces cancel eachother. By setting $|\vec{F}_{moon}| = |\vec{F}_{earth}|$, calculate the radial distance of the "balance point." From there, identify the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of the balance point. Typeset your derivation (show multiple steps!) in latex in a markdown cell below. Then, calculate it numerically, and draw the answer onto a new version of your force field plot. Again mark the location of the earth with a black circle, and the moon with a black square; mark the location of the balance point with a red x. Add a legend.

Your Derivation Goes in the markdown cell below:

${d}_{EM} = d_M + d_E$ , $ \vec{{d}_{EM}} = \vec{d_E} - \vec{d_M} $

$|\vec{F_E}|=|\vec{F_M}|$

$|\vec{F_E}| = |-G\frac{M_E m}{d_E^2} \hat{d}_{E}| = G\frac{M_E m}{d_E^2}$ , $|\vec{F_M}| = |-G\frac{M_m m}{d_M^2} \hat{d}_{M}| = G\frac{M_m m}{d_M^2}$

$=> G\frac{M_E m}{d_E^2} = G\frac{M_m m}{d_M^2}$

$-> \frac{M_E}{d_E^2} = \frac{M_m}{d_M^2}$

$ \frac{d_M^2}{d_E^2} = \frac{M_m}{M_E}$

$ \frac{d_M}{d_E} = \sqrt{\frac{M_m}{M_E}} $

$ d_M = d_E\sqrt{\frac{M_m}{M_E}}$

$ -> {d}_{EM} = d_E\sqrt{\frac{M_m}{M_E}} + d_E$

$ d_E = \frac{{d}_{EM}}{1 + \sqrt{\frac{M_m}{M_E}}} $

$ \vec{{d}_{EM}} = (\frac{{d}_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}} , \frac{{d}_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}}) $ $ -> \vec{d_E} = (\frac{{d}_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}(1 + \sqrt{\frac{M_m}{M_E}})},\frac{{d}_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}(1 + \sqrt{\frac{M_m}{M_E}})}) $

And your updated plot, including the balance point, goes in the cell below.

In [10]:
BP = d_EM/(2 + np.sqrt(2*M_M/M_E))
# not done needs to be a different type of plot
fig, ax5 = plt.subplots()


# visuals for earth and moon
ax5.plot([0], [0], "ko", label = 'earth')
ax5.plot([M_xy], [M_xy], "ks", label = 'moon')
ax5.plot([BP], [BP], "rx", label = 'balance point')

# Main plot
Stream = ax5.streamplot(xprobe,yprobe, -FEx-FMx,-FEy-FMy, color = color2, cmap='nipy_spectral')
ax5.set_aspect("equal")
cbar = fig.colorbar(Stream.lines)
fig.tight_layout()

# plot labels
ax5.legend(loc='lower right');
ax5.set_title('Vector stream plot of the gravitational force of the earth moon system')
ax5.set_ylabel('Y distance m')
ax5.set_xlabel('X distance m')
cbar.set_label('gravitational force log(N)')
Figure
  1. Quantitatively discuss your conclusions. Is the balance point close to the Moon or Earth? How close? Does this make sense?

Your Answer Goes in the markdown cell below:

The balance point is about 90% of the way from the earth to the moon, this makes sense as the earth is about 100 times heavier than the moon as such based on the relation found the distance is inversly proportional to 1 plus the sqruare root of the ratio of the masses, which is about 1 + sqrt(0.01) or 1.1. The inverse of 1.1 is about 0.90.¶

Part 4: Altitude of the Saturn V Rocket¶

Rockets function by conservation of momentum. The ejection of fuel backwards propels the rocket forwards. The change in a rocket's velocity $\Delta v$ as a function of time $t$ (under a set of simplifying assumptions) is given by the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation:

$$\Delta v(t) = v_e \ln \left(\frac{m_0}{m(t)}\right) - gt$$

where $m_0$ is the initial "wet" mass (fuel + rocket parts + payload), $m(t) = m_0 - \dot{m}t$ is the mass at time $t$, $\dot{m}$ is the fuel burn rate (which we will assume to be constant), $v_e$ is the fuel exhaust velocity, and $g$ is the gravitational acceleration.

You can find the altitude $h$ of the rocket at "burnout" (when all the fuel is used up) by integrating the velocity over time:

$$h = \int_{0}^{T} \Delta v(t) \, dt$$

where $T$ is the total burn time of the rocket, which by conservation of momentum arguments is:

$$T = \frac{m_0 - m_f}{\dot{m}}$$

where $m_f$ is the final "dry mass" of the rocket, once all the fuel is burned.

In [ ]:
  1. Calculate the burn time $T$ for the first stage of the new Saturn V rocket that NASA is planning to use to carry the Apollo capsule.
In [11]:
# Calculation for burn time T
T = (m0 - mf)/m # s
T
Out[11]:
157.69230769230768
  1. Define a function that returns the change in velocity $\Delta v(t)$. It should take all necessary arguments, meaning: the time, wet mass, dry mass, burn rate, exhaust speed, and gravitational constant $g$. Have it return the change in velocity. Note that the rocket eventually runs out of fuel, so you should design your function carefully so that $\Delta v$ eventually becomes zero after all fuel is spent. Again, document and guard your function according to best practices.
In [12]:
def delta_V(t, m_wet, m_dry, m_rate, v_exhaust, g_cons: float) -> float:
    """Function for calculating the change in velocity experienced by the rocket
    args:
    t: Time s (float)
    m_wet: initial mass of the rocket with fuel kg (float)
    m_dry: mass of rocket without fuel kg (float)
    m_rate: rate of fuel use kg/s (float)
    v_exhaust: velocity of expellant m/s (float)
    g_cons: gravitational acceleration near the surface of a planet m/s^2 (float)
    out put: 
    dv: the change in velocity of the rocket m/s (float) 
    """
    m_current = m_wet - t*m_rate
    
    if not isinstance(t, float):
        raise TypeError(f"Mass needs to be a float") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(m_wet, float):
        raise TypeError(f"Mass needs to be a float") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(m_dry, float):
        raise TypeError(f"Mass needs to be a float") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(m_rate, float):
        raise TypeError(f"Rate of mass loss needs to be a float") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(v_exhaust, float):
        raise TypeError(f"Velocity needs to be a float") # gives error
        
    elif not isinstance(g_cons, float):
        raise TypeError(f"gravitational acceleration needs to be a float") # gives error

    elif m_wet < m_dry:
        raise ValueError(f"m_wet should be larger than m_dry as fuel has mass") # gives error
        
    elif m_dry < 0: # makes sure the mass is positive
        raise ValueError(f"Mass must be positive")

    elif v_exhaust < 0: # makes sure velocity is positive
        raise ValueError(f"exhaust velocity must be positive")

    elif g_cons < 0: # makes sure gravitational acceleration is positive
        raise ValueError(f"Gravity at surface is atractive and for our use a negative value would imply repulsion")

    elif m_rate < 0: # makes sure the rate of mass loss is positive
        raise ValueError(f"negative rate implies that fuel grows when burning which is impossible")
        
    elif m_current < m_dry: # the velocity stops increasing once the fuel is all burnt through
        dv = -g_cons*t 
        
    else: # main calculation 
        dv = v_exhaust*np.log(m_wet/(m_wet - m_rate * t)) - g_cons*t
        
        # Post conditions
        assert isinstance(dv, float), " dv should be a float"
    return(dv)
  1. Compute the altitude of the rocket at the end of the burn. Do this by numerically integrating from $t=0$ (launch) to $t=T$ (burnout). Use the scipy.integrate.quad() function.
In [13]:
integral, err = quad(delta_V, 0,T, args=(m0, mf, m, ve, g))
print(integral, err)

74093.98013366401 5.848811185394151e-08
  1. Just last week, NASA got the test results back from the first prototype of Saturn V. They found that it burned for about 160 seconds and lifted the system to an altitude of about 70km. Director Kranz has asked you to compare your calculations for $T$ and $h$ to the testing data. You should offer some explanations about why your calculation is an over or under estimate, e.g. neglecting drag.

Your Answer Goes in the markdown cell below:

The calculated value for T was 157.69 s this is very close to the time found of 160s. It may have been a slight under estimate in that it assumes that the fuel is burned at a constant rate with perfect performance from the thruster.

The value I calculated for h is about 74 km this is over the observed value of 70km. My calculation was an over estimate as it didnt acount for drag which would slow down the rocket significantly in the early parts where the atmosphere is thickest. In some ways my calculation was an underestimate as it didnt acount for the force of gravity changing with distance but this under estimation is far less significant than the over estimation previusly disscussed.
